topics.title: Applications of unit operations to the food industry, analytical processing concepts with regards to mass and energy balances, equipment involved in the commercially important food processing methods and unit operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Applications of unit operations to the food industry, analytical processing concepts with regards to mass and energy balances, equipment involved in the commercially important food processing methods and unit operations # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Unit Operations – Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Operation (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *unit operation* is a fundamental, indivisible physical or chemical transformation step that is the building block of any industrial food process. When combined in a logical sequence, several unit operations form a complete **food processing line** that converts raw agricultural commodities (milk, fruits, grains, vegetables, meat) into safe, stable, marketable food products. The concept was popularized by **Arthur D. Little's "unit operations" approach** (1916), which revolutionized chemical/food engineering by reducing every complex process into standard physical/chemical operations (heating, cooling, drying, evaporation, filtration, mixing, etc.). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Kitchen-as-Factory" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are making mango jam at home. You wash the mangoes (cleaning), peel and cut them (size reduction), boil them with sugar (heat transfer + evaporation), and pour into jars (filling/packaging). Each *kitchen step* is a *unit operation*. A commercial jam factory performs the **same unit operations** but with industrial-scale *equipment* (e.g., rotary coil evaporator, plate heat exchanger, aseptic filler) governed by **mass and energy balances**. ## 1.3 Classification of Unit Operations in Food Industry | **Category** | **Driving Force / Principle** | **Typical Food Industry Examples** | |---|---|---| | **Heat Transfer Operations** | Temperature gradient ($\Delta T$) | Pasteurization of milk, sterilization of canned food, blanching of vegetables | | **Mass Transfer Operations** | Concentration gradient ($\Delta C$) | Evaporation of fruit juice, drying of grains, distillation of essential oils, extraction of sugar | | **Mechanical Operations** | Mechanical force, pressure | Size reduction (hammer mill), filtration, centrifugation, mixing | | **Momentum Transfer** | Velocity / pressure gradient | Pumping of viscous honey, fluidization of powders | ## 1.4 Mass and Energy Balances – The Bookkeeping Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Balance:** Based on the **Law of Conservation of Mass** (Lavoisier, 1789): *Matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a non-nuclear process*. In food engineering, this means *what enters the system as raw material must exit as product, by-product, or waste* – it cannot simply disappear. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Balance:** Based on the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: *Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another*. In a food process, the *energy supplied* (steam, electricity) must equal *energy leaving* (in product, in waste heat, in work) plus *accumulation* in the system. ## 1.5 Standard Reference Constants & Units > [!NOTE] > **Critical Constants Every Food Engineer Must Memorize:** > - Specific heat of water: $c_p = 4.186 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ > - Latent heat of vaporization of water at $100^\circ \text{C}$: $\lambda_{vap} = 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ > - Latent heat of fusion of ice: $\lambda_{fus} = 334 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ > - Standard atmospheric pressure: $1 \; \text{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 760 \; \text{mmHg}$ > - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ > - Standard acceleration of gravity: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$ # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Generalized Mass Balance Equation For any food processing equipment (an evaporator, drier, or mixer), the **General Mass Balance** in words is: $$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Input Rate} - \text{Output Rate} + \text{Generation Rate} - \text{Consumption Rate}$$ Mathematically: $$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$$ ### Steady-State Simplification (most food process equipment is operated at steady state) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Insight:** In steady state, $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$, and for non-reactive food processes, generation and consumption terms vanish. The balance collapses to the famous equation: > $$\boxed{\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}}$$ > This is the most commonly used mass balance in KTU food engineering problems. ## 2.2 Component Mass Balance For any specific food component (water, sugar, protein, fat, salt), the *component mass balance* must hold independently: $$\sum \dot{m}_{in} \cdot x_{i,in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} \cdot x_{i,out}$$ where $x_i$ is the **mass fraction** of component $i$. This is the *single most important equation* for solving concentration problems in evaporators, mixers, and driers. ## 2.3 The Generalized Energy Balance Equation In words: $$\text{Energy In} = \text{Energy Out} + \text{Accumulation of Energy}$$ The mathematical form: $$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{out} + \dot{W}_{in} - \dot{W}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$$ ### Steady-State, No-Work, Sensible-Heat Energy Balance (MOST COMMON KTU PROBLEM) > [!IMPORTANT] > For a continuous food heater/cooler operated at steady state with no shaft work, the energy balance reduces to: > $$\boxed{\dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in}) = \dot{Q}}$$ > This is the **fundamental "sensible heat" equation** used for milk pasteurization, juice heating, and oil cooling calculations. ### Energy Balance with Phase Change (Evaporator) For an evaporator where water is removed from food, the energy balance becomes: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot \lambda_{vap} + \dot{m}_{conc} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ The latent heat term $\lambda_{vap} = 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (at 100°C) often dominates the total energy requirement. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is a **board-exam survival kit**. Memorize these equations, units, and validity conditions before the exam. | **#** | **Equation** | **Application** | **Units** | **Validity / Boundary Condition** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Overall steady-state mass balance | kg/s | Steady state, non-reactive | | 2 | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} x_{i,in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} x_{i,out}$ | Component mass balance | kg/s | Steady state, non-reactive, single phase | | 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heating or cooling of food | kW | No phase change, constant $c_p$ | | 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{vap} \cdot \lambda_{vap}$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kW | Pure phase change at constant T | | 5 | $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat exchanger design | kW | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = area, $\Delta T_{lm}$ = LMTD | | 6 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K or °C | Used in counter/cocurrent heat exchangers | | 7 | $R_{vapor} = \frac{\dot{m}_{vapor}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$ | Vapor-to-feed ratio in evaporator | – | Dimensionless | | 8 | $\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{supplied}}$ | Thermal efficiency of dryer/heater | % | $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$ | | 9 | $Moisture \, Content \, (\% wb) = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | Wet-basis moisture in drying | % | $m_w$ = water mass, $m_s$ = solid mass | | 10 | $Moisture \, Content \, (\% db) = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | Dry-basis moisture in drying | % | Used in drying rate curves | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students confuse **wet basis (%wb)** and **dry basis (%db)** moisture content. The difference can be 5–10% for typical foods and leads to *direct mark deductions*. Use %wb for reporting (industry standard) and %db for engineering drying calculations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy Auditing:** Mass and energy balances allow food factories to *quantify steam, water, and electricity consumption* per kg of product – essential for **ISO 50001 Energy Management** compliance. - **Process Optimization:** Balances reveal *bottleneck streams* (e.g., the evaporator vapor in a tomato paste plant) where energy can be recovered through **Multiple Effect Evaporation** or **Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR)**. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Accurate balances are required for **FSSAI (India)** and **FDA** traceability, nutritional labeling (mass of protein, fat, carbohydrate), and environmental discharge reporting. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – The Overall Mass Balance from First Principles Starting from the conservation of mass for an open, flowing system (the control volume shown in Section 4): The total mass entering the control volume in time $dt$ is $\dot{m}_{in} \cdot dt$. The total mass leaving is $\dot{m}_{out} \cdot dt$. The net change in system mass is therefore: $$dm_{sys} = \dot{m}_{in} \, dt - \dot{m}_{out} \, dt + \dot{m}_{gen} \, dt - \dot{m}_{cons} \, dt$$ Dividing through by $dt$: $$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$$ For a **food process with no chemical reaction** (e.g., evaporation of water from milk, drying of rice), $\dot{m}_{gen} = 0$ and $\dot{m}_{cons} = 0$: $$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$ For **continuous steady-state operation**, $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$, giving: $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** This elegant one-line equation is the starting point for *every* food process design problem on the KTU exam. All subsequent "find the concentration/flow of the product" problems reduce to this balance plus a component balance. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) Consider a counter-current heat exchanger (e.g., milk being pasteurized by hot water). Let: - Hot fluid enters at $T_{h,in}$, leaves at $T_{h,out}$ - Cold fluid enters at $T_{c,in}$, leaves at $T_{c,out}$ For a differential element of area $dA$ at position $x$, the local temperature difference is $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$. The differential heat transfer rate is: $$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T(x)$$ Assuming both fluids have constant $c_p$ and no phase change, $dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} dT_c$. Integrating across the full exchanger area $A$ from end 1 ($\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$) to end 2 ($\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\frac{U A}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} \int_{0}^{A} dA$$ Evaluating both integrals (with a sign change for proper orientation): $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right) = \frac{U A}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}$$ Solving for total $Q$ via the overall energy balance $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ and substituting the result for $A$: $$\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why LMTD and not AMTD (arithmetic mean)?** The temperature profile along a heat exchanger is **exponential**, not linear. The arithmetic mean of inlet and outlet temperature differences can be off by 20–40% for real milk/water systems. The LMTD is the *exact* integral of the exponential profile, hence its dominance in KTU problems. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Typical KTU Food Process Equipment | **Equipment** | **Primary Unit Operation** | **Governing Balance** | **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | Heat transfer (pasteurization) | $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Pasteurization efficiency $\eta$ = % of flow above 72°C for 15 s | | **Multiple-Effect Evaporator** | Vaporization (juice concentration) | $\dot{m}_{vap} \lambda_{vap} = \dot{m}_{steam} \lambda_{steam} \times N_{effects}$ | Steam Economy = kg water evaporated per kg steam | | **Spray Dryer** | Combined heat + mass transfer (milk powder) | $\dot{m}_{air} c_p \Delta T + \dot{m}_{air} Y \lambda_{vap} = \dot{Q}_{supplied}$ | Thermal efficiency = useful heat for water removal / total heat input | | **Centrifuge (Cream Separator)** | Mechanical separation | $\dot{m}_{inlet} = \dot{m}_{cream} + \dot{m}_{skim}$ | Separation efficiency = % fat removed in cream | | **Hammer Mill** | Size reduction (flour, spices) | Power = $\dot{m} \cdot E_{specific}$ (Bond's law) | Particle size distribution (PSD) | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example – Milk Pasteurizer (Steady-State Energy Balance):** > > Milk ($\dot{m} = 5000 \; \text{kg/h}$) enters a plate heat exchanger at $T_{in} = 4^\circ\text{C}$ and exits at $T_{out} = 72^\circ\text{C}$ for pasteurization. The heating medium is hot water entering at $T_{h,in} = 95^\circ\text{C}$ and leaving at $T_{h,out} = 80^\circ\text{C}$. Take $c_p^{milk} = 3.9 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $c_p^{water} = 4.18 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. > > **Step 1 – Heat required by milk (sensible heat):** > $$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \cdot c_p^{milk} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ > $$Q = \left(\frac{5000}{3600}\right) \cdot 3.9 \cdot (72 - 4) = 1.389 \cdot 3.9 \cdot 68$$ > $$Q = 368.4 \; \text{kW}$$ > > **Step 2 – Hot water flow rate (energy balance on water side):** > $$Q = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot c_p^{water} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$ > $$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{368.4}{4.18 \cdot (95 - 80)} = \frac{368.4}{62.7}$$ > $$\dot{m}_{water} = 5.876 \; \text{kg/s} \approx 21{,}154 \; \text{kg/h}$$ > > **Step 3 – Verify with LMTD (design check):** > $$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 95 - 72 = 23^\circ\text{C}$$ > $$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 4 = 76^\circ\text{C}$$ > $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{23 - 76}{\ln(23/76)} = \frac{-53}{\ln(0.3026)} = \frac{-53}{-1.196} \approx 44.3^\circ\text{C}$$ > $$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$ > For a typical PHE with $U = 2500 \; \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$: > $$A = \frac{368{,}400}{2500 \cdot 44.3} \approx 3.33 \; \text{m}^2$$ > > This is a realistic plate heat exchanger size for a 5000 kg/h dairy plant. ✓ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Generic Food Processing Plant Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["RAW MATERIAL INTAKE
Fruits Milk Grains Meat"] nodeB["UNIT OPERATION 1
CLEANING AND SORTING
Mass Balance Applied"] nodeC["UNIT OPERATION 2
SIZE REDUCTION
Mechanical Energy Balance"] nodeD["UNIT OPERATION 3
HEAT TRANSFER
Pasteurization Blanching"] nodeE["UNIT OPERATION 4
MASS TRANSFER
Evaporation Drying"] nodeF["UNIT OPERATION 5
SEPARATION
Filtration Centrifugation"] nodeG["UNIT OPERATION 6
PACKAGING
Filling Sealing"] nodeH["FINISHED PRODUCT OUT
Safe Stable Marketable"] nodeI["MASS BALANCE LOOP
In equals Out plus Waste"] nodeJ["ENERGY BALANCE LOOP
Steam Electricity plus Heat Recovery"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeB nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeC nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeD nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeE nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeF nodeI -.Monitors.-> nodeG nodeJ -.Supplies.-> nodeD nodeJ -.Supplies.-> nodeE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the Flowchart:** The diagram shows a *typical linear food processing line* (raw material → finished product). The two **dashed loops** represent the **mass balance** and **energy balance** monitoring systems that *envelop* every unit operation. In an industrial plant, this is implemented as a **SCADA / DCS** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition / Distributed Control System) dashboard that continuously tracks $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ and $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out}$. ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic – Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) Internal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP1["HOT FLUID IN
T equal 95C"] nodeP2["Plate 1
Hot Channel"] nodeP3["Plate 2
Cold Channel"] nodeP4["Plate 3
Hot Channel"] nodeP5["Plate 4
Cold Channel"] nodeP6["HOT FLUID OUT
T equal 80C"] nodeP7["COLD FLUID IN
T equal 4C Milk"] nodeP8["COLD FLUID OUT
T equal 72C Pasteurized"] nodeP1 --> nodeP2 --> nodeP4 --> nodeP6 nodeP7 --> nodeP3 --> nodeP5 --> nodeP8 nodeP2 -.Heat Transfer.-> nodeP3 nodeP3 -.Heat Transfer.-> nodeP4 nodeP4 -.Heat Transfer.-> nodeP5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** The arrows show the *flow paths* of the two fluids. The dotted arrows show the *heat transfer direction* through the plate walls. In a real PHE, 20–200 corrugated stainless-steel plates are clamped in a frame, with hot and cold channels alternating to maximize the heat transfer area in a compact volume. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Spray Dryer ```mermaid flowchart TB sda["CONCENTRATED FEED
Milk 50 percent solids"] sdb["ATOMIZER
Rotary Disc or Nozzle"] sdc["DRYING CHAMBER
Hot Air 180C"] sdd["PARTICLE FORMATION
Moisture Evaporates"] sde["CYCLONE SEPARATOR
Powder Recovery"] sdf["PRODUCT OUT
Milk Powder 4 percent moisture"] sdg["EXHAUST AIR
Water Vapor 90C"] sdh["AIR HEATER
Steam or Gas"] sdi["FRESH AIR IN
T equal 25C"] sda --> sdb --> sdd --> sde --> sdf sdi --> sdh --> sdc sdc --> sdg ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The spray dryer combines **three** unit operations in one vessel: (1) *atomization* (mechanical), (2) *heat transfer* from hot air to droplets, and (3) *mass transfer* of water vapor from droplet to air. The mass and energy balances must be solved **simultaneously** for the air stream and the product stream – a typical 14-mark KTU problem. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > *As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part A consists of short-answer questions testing definitional and conceptual understanding. Each carries 3 marks.* ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Define the term "Unit Operation" as applied to food processing. List any four important unit operations used in the food industry with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > A *unit operation* is a basic physical or chemical transformation step that, when combined in a sequence, constitutes a complete industrial food process. The concept was introduced by Arthur D. Little to provide a unified engineering framework across diverse food products. > > **Four Important Unit Operations:** > 1. **Heat Transfer (Pasteurization):** Heating milk to 72°C for 15 s to destroy pathogens. > 2. **Evaporation (Concentration):** Removing water from fruit juice to produce concentrate. > 3. **Drying (Dehydration):** Reducing moisture in grains, fruits, or vegetables to enhance shelf life. > 4. **Mechanical Separation (Filtration/Centrifugation):** Separating cream from milk using a centrifugal separator. *(3 marks – 1 for definition, 2 for examples)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary] **State the law of conservation of mass. Write the general mass balance equation and explain the meaning of each term.** **Model Answer:** > The **Law of Conservation of Mass**, first stated by Antoine Lavoisier (1789), asserts that *matter can neither be created nor destroyed in any non-nuclear physical or chemical process*. The total mass of a closed system remains constant. > > **General Mass Balance Equation:** > $$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$$ > > Where: > - $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt}$ = rate of mass accumulation within the system (kg/s) > - $\dot{m}_{in}$ = mass flow rate entering the system (kg/s) > - $\dot{m}_{out}$ = mass flow rate leaving the system (kg/s) > - $\dot{m}_{gen}$ = mass generation rate (e.g., by chemical reaction, 0 for pure physical food processes) > - $\dot{m}_{cons}$ = mass consumption rate (e.g., by chemical reaction, 0 for pure physical food processes) *(3 marks – 1 for law, 1 for equation, 1 for explanation of terms)* --- ## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based) > [!NOTE] > *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B features 14-mark questions with internal choice. Each question is split into (a) and (b) sub-parts, usually 7 marks each.* ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Multiple Effect Evaporator used in the food industry. State its advantages over a single-effect evaporator. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is a series arrangement of evaporator bodies (effects) where the vapor generated in one effect serves as the heating medium for the next effect, operating at progressively lower pressure. This drastically reduces fresh steam consumption. **Working Principle:** - **First Effect:** Live steam (e.g., at 2.5 bar, ~127°C) heats the feed. The vapor generated in this effect is at ~120°C. - **Second Effect:** The vapor from the first effect becomes the heating medium in the second effect, which is maintained at lower pressure (~1 bar), allowing it to boil at ~100°C. - **Third Effect:** Vapor from the second effect heats the third effect at even lower pressure, and so on. - The last effect is connected to a **vacuum system** (e.g., a barometric condenser). **Steam Economy Calculation:** For an $N$-effect evaporator, theoretically, **1 kg of steam evaporates N kg of water** (Steam Economy = $N$). In practice, due to heat losses, economy is $0.7N$ to $0.85N$. **Advantages over Single-Effect:** 1. **Reduced Steam Consumption:** Steam economy increases 2–5 times, drastically cutting fuel/energy cost. 2. **Better Steam Economy:** Ideal for large-scale juice and dairy plants (tomato paste, fruit concentrate). 3. **Lower Operating Temperature in Later Effects:** Heat-sensitive foods (enzymes, vitamins) suffer less thermal damage in later effects. 4. **Energy Recovery:** Cascading vapor uses latent heat repeatedly. *(7 marks – 2 for working, 2 for sketch description, 3 for advantages)* **(b) A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate $10{,}000 \; \text{kg/h}$ of tomato juice from $5\%$ to $25\%$ total solids. The evaporator operates at $100^\circ\text{C}$ with saturated steam at $1.5 \; \text{bar}$ (latent heat $\lambda_s = 2226 \; \text{kJ/kg}$). The feed enters at $30^\circ\text{C}$ and the concentrated product leaves at $100^\circ\text{C}$. Specific heat of the juice (variable, use $c_p = 4.0 - 2.5 x_s$, where $x_s$ is solids mass fraction) may be approximated as $c_p^{avg} = 3.85 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Assume boiling point elevation is negligible. Calculate: (i) the rate of water evaporated, (ii) the steam consumption, and (iii) the steam economy. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Mass of water evaporated (Mass Balance):** Overall mass balance: $\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{product} + \dot{m}_{vapor}$ $$10{,}000 = \dot{m}_{product} + \dot{m}_{vapor}$$ Solids mass balance (solids are conserved, no loss): $$10{,}000 \times 0.05 = \dot{m}_{product} \times 0.25$$ $$\dot{m}_{product} = \frac{500}{0.25} = 2000 \; \text{kg/h}$$ Therefore: $$\dot{m}_{vapor} = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000 \; \text{kg/h}$$ **(i) Water evaporated = 8000 kg/h** ✓ **Step 2 – Energy Balance to find steam requirement:** The energy balance on the evaporator (neglecting heat losses): $$\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_s = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot \lambda_{vap} + \dot{m}_{product} \cdot c_p^{avg} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ Note: The product enters as part of the feed at $30^\circ\text{C}$, so the feed sensible heating is included. We can use the product (which is 2000 kg/h at $100^\circ\text{C}$) and feed (10,000 kg/h at $30^\circ\text{C}$) for the sensible heat. The total sensible heat of feed heating from $30^\circ\text{C}$ to $100^\circ\text{C}$: $$Q_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot c_p^{feed} \cdot (T_{boil} - T_{in})$$ $$Q_{sensible} = \left(\frac{10{,}000}{3600}\right) \cdot 3.85 \cdot (100 - 30) = 2.778 \cdot 3.85 \cdot 70 = 748.5 \; \text{kW}$$ The latent heat of vaporization of water at 100°C: $$Q_{vap} = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot \lambda_{vap} = \left(\frac{8000}{3600}\right) \cdot 2257 = 2.222 \cdot 2257 = 5015.7 \; \text{kW}$$ Total heat required: $$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{vap} = 748.5 + 5015.7 = 5764.2 \; \text{kW}$$ Steam mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_s} = \frac{5764.2}{2226} = 2.589 \; \text{kg/s} = 9319 \; \text{kg/h}$$ **(ii) Steam consumption ≈ 9319 kg/h** ✓ **Step 3 – Steam Economy:** $$\eta_{steam} = \frac{\dot{m}_{vapor}}{\dot{m}_{steam}} = \frac{8000}{9319} = 0.859$$ **(iii) Steam Economy ≈ 0.86 kg water / kg steam** ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Part B (b):** > Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by (a) forgetting to include the **sensible heat** of the feed heating from 30°C to 100°C (a common error is to only count the latent heat), and (b) confusing whether to use $c_p$ of feed, product, or average. For KTU, *show every step with units*; partial marks are awarded liberally for correct methodology. --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the working of a Spray Dryer used in the food industry. List the engineering and quality parameters that determine its performance. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **Spray Dryer** converts a liquid food (milk, coffee, juice) into a dry powder by atomizing the liquid into a hot air stream. It is the **workhorse of food powder production** (instant coffee, milk powder, egg powder, spice powders). **Stages of Operation:** 1. **Feed Preparation:** Concentrated liquid (e.g., 50% solids milk) is pumped to the atomizer. 2. **Atomization:** Liquid is broken into fine droplets ($10–200 \; \mu\text{m}$ diameter) using either a **rotary disc atomizer** (centrifugal force) or a **pressure nozzle** (hydraulic pressure). 3. **Drying Chamber Contact:** Droplets meet hot air ($150–200^\circ\text{C}$) in a large cylindrical chamber. Drying occurs in three phases: (i) constant rate (surface moisture), (ii) first falling rate, (iii) second falling rate (bound moisture). 4. **Particle Separation:** Dry particles are separated from exhaust air using a **cyclone separator** or **bag filter**. 5. **Cooling and Packaging:** Powder is cooled to 20–25°C before aseptic packaging. **Performance Parameters:** - **Engineering:** Inlet/outlet air temperature, air humidity, feed flow rate, atomizer speed/pressure, residence time, thermal efficiency. - **Quality:** Moisture content, particle size distribution, bulk density, solubility index, wettability, color, microbiological load. *(7 marks – 2 for flow diagram description, 3 for working stages, 2 for performance parameters)* **(b) A counter-current rotary dryer is used to dry $2000 \; \text{kg/h}$ of wet grain from $20\%$ to $2\%$ moisture (wet basis). The drying air enters at $120^\circ\text{C}$ with a humidity ratio of $0.010 \; \text{kg water/kg dry air}$ and leaves at $80^\circ\text{C}$ with a humidity ratio of $0.025 \; \text{kg water/kg dry air}$. The grain enters at $25^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $70^\circ\text{C}$. Specific heat of dry grain = $1.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Latent heat of vaporization at average air temperature = $2400 \; \text{kJ/kg}$. Calculate: (i) the rate of water evaporated, (ii) the dry air required, and (iii) the thermal efficiency of the dryer. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Water Evaporated (Mass Balance):** Convert wet basis to dry basis: - Initial moisture ($M_1$, dry basis): $M_1 = \frac{20}{100-20} \times 100 = 25\%$ - Final moisture ($M_2$, dry basis): $M_2 = \frac{2}{100-2} \times 100 = 2.04\%$ Dry solid in feed (constant throughout): $m_s = 2000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1600 \; \text{kg/h}$ Mass of feed at inlet with $M_1 = 25\%$ db: $m_{feed} = 2000 \; \text{kg/h}$ (given) Mass of product at outlet with $M_2 = 2.04\%$ db: $$m_{product} = m_s \times (1 + M_2) = 1600 \times 1.0204 = 1632.6 \; \text{kg/h}$$ **(i) Rate of water evaporated:** $$\dot{m}_{vapor} = 2000 - 1632.6 = 367.4 \; \text{kg/h}$$ **Step 2 – Dry Air Required (Air-Side Mass Balance):** The dry air flow rate is constant. Water picked up by air = water evaporated by grain. $$\dot{m}_{da} \times (Y_{out} - Y_{in}) = \dot{m}_{vapor}$$ $$\dot{m}_{da} \times (0.025 - 0.010) = 367.4 \; \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{da} \times 0.015 = 367.4$$ $$\dot{m}_{da} = 24{,}493 \; \text{kg dry air/h}$$ **(ii) Dry air required ≈ 24,493 kg/h** ✓ **Step 3 – Thermal Efficiency of Dryer:** The useful heat is the heat that actually went into: (a) Evaporating water from the grain, and (b) Heating the dry grain from 25°C to 70°C. Total useful heat: $$Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_{vapor} \cdot \lambda_{vap} + m_s \cdot c_{p,grain} \cdot (T_{g,out} - T_{g,in})$$ $$Q_{useful} = \left(\frac{367.4}{3600}\right) \cdot 2400 + \left(\frac{1600}{3600}\right) \cdot 1.5 \cdot (70 - 25)$$ $$Q_{useful} = 0.1021 \cdot 2400 + 0.4444 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 45$$ $$Q_{useful} = 245.0 + 30.0 = 275.0 \; \text{kW}$$ Total heat supplied by the air (sensible heat of air from 120°C to 80°C, plus moisture of inlet air): $$Q_{supplied} = \dot{m}_{da} \cdot (1 + Y_{in}) \cdot c_{p,moist\,air} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$ Using $c_{p,moist\,air} \approx 1.005 + 1.88 Y_{in} = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.010 = 1.024 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$: $$Q_{supplied} = \left(\frac{24{,}493}{3600}\right) \cdot (1 + 0.010) \cdot 1.024 \cdot (120 - 80)$$ $$Q_{supplied} = 6.804 \cdot 1.010 \cdot 1.024 \cdot 40$$ $$Q_{supplied} = 281.5 \; \text{kW}$$ **(iii) Thermal efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{supplied}} = \frac{275.0}{281.5} = 0.977 = 97.7\%$$ **Note:** This is an *unrealistically high* efficiency for a real rotary dryer (typical 40–60%) because we ignored heat losses to the environment and exhaust gas sensible heat. In an exam, however, the algebraic answer is the expected solution. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Question B (b):** > Common mistakes causing **2–4 mark deductions:** > 1. **Mixing %wb and %db** in drying problems – *always* convert to dry basis for engineering calculations. > 2. Forgetting the **sensible heat of the grain** ($m_s c_p \Delta T$) – the latent heat alone underestimates $Q_{useful}$ by 10–15%. > 3. Using **enthalpy of dry air** instead of **enthalpy of moist air** ($1.005 + 1.88Y$). This is a frequent error and is a *direct mark-loss trap*. > 4. Not specifying that the answer for $\dot{m}_{da}$ is *dry air*, not *moist air*. Always label units. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Bullet Summary – Memorize Before Exam Day:** - **Unit Operation** = basic physical/chemical step in a food process; introduced by Arthur D. Little (1916). - **Five Major Classes of Food Unit Operations:** (1) Heat transfer, (2) Mass transfer, (3) Mechanical/Size reduction, (4) Separation, (5) Mixing/Forming. - **Law of Conservation of Mass:** *Matter is neither created nor destroyed.* General mass balance: $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$. - **Steady-State Mass Balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ (no accumulation, no reaction in physical food processes). - **Component Mass Balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} x_{i,in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} x_{i,out}$ – the workhorse equation for concentration/mixing problems. - **First Law of Thermodynamics:** $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out}$ at steady state. - **Sensible Heat Equation:** $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ – use $c_p^{water} = 4.186 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_p^{milk} \approx 3.9$, $c_p^{air} \approx 1.005$. - **Latent Heat of Vaporization:** $\lambda_{vap} = 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ at 100°C; **Latent Heat of Fusion:** $\lambda_{fus} = 334 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ for water. - **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):** $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$; $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$. - **Evaporator Steam Economy:** $\eta = \frac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg steam consumed}}$; for N-effect evaporator, ideal = $N$. - **Drying Efficiency:** $\eta_{dryer} = \frac{Q_{latent} + Q_{sensible,grain}}{Q_{supplied,air}}$. - **%wb vs %db Moisture:** Use **%wb for reporting**; use **%db for engineering**; conversion: $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$. - **Spray Dryer:** Atomization + co-current hot air contact + cyclone separation → instant powders. - **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE):** Vapor from effect $i$ heats effect $i+1$ at lower pressure → steam economy increases linearly with number of effects. - **Major Industrial Equipment in Food Industry:** PHE, MEE, spray dryer, rotary dryer, hammer mill, decanter centrifuge, plate filter, scraped surface heat exchanger, aseptic filler. - **Engineering utility of balances:** Process design, energy audit (ISO 50001), FSSAI labeling, bottleneck identification, heat recovery (MVR, heat exchangers). - **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Mixing wb/db; ignoring sensible heat; using dry air cp instead of moist air cp; forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s in $Q$ equations.

topics.title: Value addition to cereals like rice and wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Value addition to cereals like rice and wheat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Addition in Cereals** is defined as the process of transforming raw cereal grains (such as rice, wheat, maize, oats, barley, and millets) through a series of primary, secondary, and tertiary processing operations into refined, shelf-stable, convenient, nutritionally enhanced, and economically valuable food products that command a higher market price and consumer acceptance than the original commodity.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the term **"value-added cereal products"** encompasses any cereal-derived food item that has undergone deliberate physical, chemical, biochemical, thermal, or mechanical transformation to enhance one or more of the following attributes:

- **Nutritional Profile** (fortification, protein enrichment, fiber addition)
- **Functional Properties** (gelatinization, gel formation, emulsification)
- **Shelf Life** (moisture reduction, packaging, preservation)
- **Convenience** (ready-to-eat, ready-to-cook, instant mixes)
- **Sensory Appeal** (texture, flavor, color, aroma)
- **Economic Value** (higher price per unit mass, broader market reach)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Raw Mango → Pickle" Analogy**
> Imagine a sack of raw mangoes sold at ₹20/kg. Pickle the same mangoes with oil, salt, spices, and preservatives, and the same mango now sells at ₹300/kg. The mango did not change — but its *value* did. Cereals behave identically: a kilogram of paddy (rough rice) selling at ₹22/kg becomes ₹80/kg as **basmati rice**, ₹250/kg as **rice flakes (poha)**, and ₹600/kg as **infant rice cereal**. Value addition is the *engineering of transformation* — converting low-margin agricultural commodities into high-margin engineered foods.

The **"value ladder"** in cereal processing ascends as follows:

1. **Level 0 — Raw Grain** (lowest value, highest volume)
2. **Level 1 — Primary Processing** (cleaning, dehusking, milling)
3. **Level 2 — Secondary Processing** (flour, semolina, flakes, puffing)
4. **Level 3 — Tertiary Processing** (bakery, pasta, extruded snacks, breakfast cereals)
5. **Level 4 — Convenience Engineering** (instant mixes, RTE foods, fortified products)

### 1.3 Cereal Classification (KTU Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Cereal grains are monocotyledonous starchy seeds belonging to the **Poaceae (Gramineae) family**. The three major structural components — **endosperm (82–84%)**, **bran (13–14%)**, and **germ (2–3%)** — form the anatomical basis of all value-addition strategies.

| Cereal Group | Examples | Key Value-Added Products | Dominant Starch Type |
|--------------|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Rice (Oryza sativa)** | Basmati, Sona Masuri, Brown rice | Parboiled rice, Poha, Murmura, Rice flour, RTE flakes | Amylose (20–25%) |
| **Wheat (Triticum aestivum)** | Durum, Hard, Soft | Atta, Maida, Suji, Bread, Biscuits, Pasta | Amylopectin-rich gluten network |
| **Maize (Zea mays)** | Dent, Flint, Sweet corn | Cornflakes, Popcorn, Starch, Tortilla | Waxy/normal starch |
| **Millets** | Ragi, Jowar, Bajra | Malt, Porridge, Flakes, Cookies | Small granule starch |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cereal Grain — Anatomical Foundation for Value Addition

Every value-addition strategy is dictated by the **three-tier anatomy** of a cereal kernel. Understanding this is critical for KTU Part B answers.

**Layer 1 — Husk/Hull (0–20% by weight)**
- Inedible outer covering in rice, barley, oats
- Removed during **dehulling/dehusking**
- Function: protects against moisture, insects, microbial attack

**Layer 2 — Bran (13–14% in wheat, 5–6% in rice after polishing)**
- Composed of **aleurone layer, pericarp, testa**
- Rich in **dietary fiber (cellulose, hemicellulose), B-vitamins, minerals (Fe, Zn, Mg), phytochemicals, antioxidants**
- In wheat: removed during refined flour production → nutritional loss
- In rice: removed during polishing → white rice
- **Value-addition angle**: bran oil extraction, bran-based fiber supplements, defatted bran in bakery

**Layer 3 — Endosperm (80–84%)**
- The **energy storage tissue** of the grain
- Composed primarily of **starch granules (72%)** embedded in a **protein matrix (8–14%)**
- In wheat: contains **gluten-forming proteins (gliadin + glutenin)**
- **Value-addition angle**: starch modification, hydrolysis, gelatinization, extrusion

**Layer 4 — Germ/Embryo (2–3%)**
- Oil-rich, vitamin E abundant
- Highly perishable due to lipase activity
- Often removed in refined products to extend shelf life
- **Value-addition angle**: germ oil extraction (wheat germ oil, rice bran oil), vitamin supplements

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Cereal Value Addition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Primary Processing (Cleaning & Milling)**
> **Pillar 2 — Secondary Processing (Flour/Flake/Puff Production)**
> **Pillar 3 — Tertiary Processing (Product Formulation)**

#### Pillar 1: Primary Processing Operations

| Operation | Technical Definition | Effect on Grain | Value-Added Output |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Cleaning** | Removal of foreign matter (stones, chaff, dust, weed seeds) | Purity ↑ | Cleaned grain (15–25% price premium) |
| **Dehusking/Hulling** | Mechanical removal of outer husk | Edibility ↑ | Brown rice / Husked wheat |
| **Conditioning/Tempering** | Moisture adjustment (14–16% for wheat, 12–14% for rice) | Milling efficiency ↑ | Optimized mill yield (2–4% extra flour) |
| **Milling/Polishing** | Gradual reduction through roller/pounding | Bran/germ removed | White rice, Maida, Atta |
| **Parboiling** | Hydrothermal treatment (soak → steam → dry) | Nutrient migration to endosperm | Parboiled rice (Sella/USNA) |
| **Sorting/Grading** | Optical, density, or size-based separation | Uniformity ↑ | Premium graded rice (Basmati, Sona Masuri) |

#### Pillar 2: Secondary Processing Operations

**Rice-Based Secondary Products:**

1. **Rice Flakes (Poha / Chiura / Avalakki)**
   - Process: Soaking paddy → roasting → flattening
   - Unit operation: **gelatinization + mechanical flattening**
   - Value: 3–5× raw rice price
   - Industry size: India is world's largest producer (~₹5,000 crore industry)

2. **Puffed Rice (Murmura / Kurmura)**
   - Process: Sand/salt roasting at **230–250 °C** causing rapid moisture vaporization
   - Physics: Moisture content drops from ~14% to ~3% during puffing
   - Expansion ratio: **8–12×** (volumetric)

3. **Rice Flour**
   - Produced by dry milling or wet grinding
   - Applications: gluten-free bakery, infant foods, extruded snacks, batter mixes
   - Granulometry: 80–200 mesh

4. **Rice Bran Oil (RBO)**
   - Extracted from stabilized rice bran using **hexane solvent (n-hexane, BP 68.7 °C)** or expeller pressing
   - Yield: **18–22% oil** from stabilized bran
   - Premium oil due to **γ-oryzanol (1.2–1.8%)**, **tocotrienols**, and balanced omega-6:omega-3 ratio

5. **Parboiled Rice (Sella Rice)**
   - Process: Soak (50–70 °C, 3–4 hrs) → Steam (15–20 min at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm²) → Dry (moisture 13–14%) → Mill
   - **Nutrient migration**: ~80% of water-soluble B-vitamins diffuse from bran to endosperm
   - **Hardness**: 2–3× harder than raw rice → less breakage during milling (head rice yield 65–70% vs 55%)

**Wheat-Based Secondary Products:**

1. **Whole Wheat Flour (Atta / Chapatti Flour)**
   - Extraction rate: **95–98%** (includes bran)
   - Particle size: 150–250 microns
   - Used for chapati, roti, paratha

2. **Refined Wheat Flour (Maida)**
   - Extraction rate: **65–70%** (endosperm only)
   - Particle size: 80–120 microns
   - Bleaching agents: benzoyl peroxide (40 ppm max under FSSAI)
   - Applications: biscuits, cakes, bread, naan, pastries

3. **Semolina (Suji / Rava)**
   - Coarse granular product from durum wheat
   - Particle size: 250–500 microns
   - Applications: halwa, upma, pasta, couscous

4. **Wheat Germ Oil**
   - Cold-pressed from wheat germ
   - Rich in **vitamin E (tocopherols ~2500 mg/kg)**
   - Premium cosmetic and nutraceutical applications

5. **Bran-Based Products**
   - Wheat bran: fiber supplement, breakfast cereal inclusion
   - Defatted bran: bakery, extruded products
   - Prebiotic potential due to **arabinoxylan** content

#### Pillar 3: Tertiary Processing & Formulated Products

1. **Bakery Products** — bread, biscuits, cakes, cookies
2. **Pasta & Noodles** — spaghetti, macaroni, instant noodles
3. **Breakfast Cereals** — cornflakes, muesli, granola
4. **Extruded Snacks** — puffs, curls, third-generation snacks
5. **Ready-to-Eat (RTE) Foods** — idli/dosa mixes, upma mixes
6. **Infant & Weaning Foods** — fortified blended foods, cerelac-type products
7. **Fermented Cereal Products** — idli, dosa batter, tempeh, sourdough

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derived, industry-standard, and KTU-verifiable**. They are essential for numerical problems in Part B (14-mark) and Part A (3-mark) questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Milling Recovery / Yield** | $\text{Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{M_{\text{clean grain out}}}{M_{\text{raw grain in}}} \times 100$ | Rice: 60–70%, Wheat: 70–78% | Process economics |
| 2 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $HRY = \dfrac{M_{\text{whole kernels}}}{M_{\text{paddy in}}} \times 100$ | Premium Basmati: 60–65% | Milling efficiency |
| 3 | **Extraction Rate (Wheat Flour)** | $\text{ER (\%)} = \dfrac{M_{\text{flour}}}{M_{\text{clean wheat}}} \times 100$ | Atta: 95–98%, Maida: 65–70% | Flour classification |
| 4 | **Moisture Loss on Drying** | $\Delta M = \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f/100}$ | Parboiling: 30% → 13% | Mass balance in drying |
| 5 | **Puffing Expansion Ratio** | $ER_{\text{puff}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{puffed}}}{V_{\text{raw}}}$ | Murmura: 8–12× | Texture evaluation |
| 6 | **Water Absorption Index (WAI)** | $WAI = \dfrac{M_{\text{gel}}}{M_{\text{dry sample}}}$ | Rice: 3.5–5.0, Wheat: 5.0–7.0 | Starch functionality |
| 7 | **Water Solubility Index (WSI)** | $WSI = \dfrac{M_{\text{dissolved solids}}}{M_{\text{dry sample}} \times \text{dry basis}}$ | Extruded rice: 10–35% | Starch degradation |
| 8 | **Gelatinization Temperature** | $T_g$ | Rice: 68–78 °C, Wheat: 58–65 °C | Cooking/Extrusion design |
| 9 | **Starch Retrogradation Rate** | $\text{Rate} \propto e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Avrami equation | Staling of bread |
| 10 | **Gluten Yield** | $Y_g = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet gluten}}}{M_{\text{flour}}} \times 100$ | Hard wheat: 25–35% | Bread-making quality |
| 11 | **Bran Oil Yield** | $\eta_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{bran}}} \times 100$ | Rice bran: 18–22% | Solvent extraction |
| 12 | **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $PER = \dfrac{\Delta M_{\text{body}}}{M_{\text{protein consumed}}}$ | Wheat: 1.5–2.0, Rice: 1.7–2.2 | Nutritional labeling |
| 13 | **Degree of Gelatinization (DG)** | $DG = \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}} - A_{\text{raw}}}{A_{\text{fully cooked}} - A_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$ | Extrusion >85% | Extrusion control |
| 14 | **Glass Transition Temperature** | $T_g = T_g^0 - \frac{k \cdot M_w \cdot M_n}{M_w + M_n}$ | Rice: ~30 °C (low moisture) | Storage stability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice parboiling plants** in India contribute to **30% of rice exports** worth ~₹40,000 crore.
- **Wheat milling** produces **~80 million tonnes** of flour globally per year.
- **Extrusion technology** converts broken rice (low value) into **3rd generation snacks** (high value) — the **one-rupee input becomes ten-rupee output**.
- **Rice bran oil** is now positioned as a **"health oil"** globally, with India producing ~10 lakh tonnes annually.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Milling Recovery & Mass Balance for Wheat Flour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context (KTU-typical 7-mark problem):**
> A wheat milling unit processes **10,000 kg of clean wheat** per day. The mill produces **6,800 kg of maida (refined flour)**, **2,400 kg of atta + suji combined**, and **800 kg of bran + germ loss**. Calculate: (a) Maida extraction rate, (b) Total flour yield, (c) Atta:suji ratio if atta is 60% of the secondary product stream.

#### Step 1 — Set Up the Mass Balance Equation

The fundamental mass balance for wheat milling is:

$$
M_{\text{clean wheat}} = M_{\text{maida}} + M_{\text{atta + suji}} + M_{\text{bran + germ}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
M_{\text{clean wheat}} = 6800 + 2400 + 800 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(Verified)}
$$

**Textual logic:** The total mass entering the mill must equal the total mass leaving the mill (Law of Conservation of Mass). The given values satisfy this equation, confirming the data is internally consistent.

#### Step 2 — Calculate the Maida Extraction Rate (ER)

Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
\text{ER}_{\text{maida}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{maida}}}{M_{\text{clean wheat}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ER}_{\text{maida}} = \dfrac{6800}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 68\%
$$

**Textual logic:** This means 68% of the cleaned wheat is converted into refined maida. Industry standard for maida is **65–70%**, so this mill is operating within optimal parameters.

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Total Flour Yield

Total flour (edible products) = Maida + Atta + Suji (bran is not edible flour):

$$
M_{\text{total flour}} = 6800 + 2400 = 9200 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Total yield (\%)} = \dfrac{9200}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 92\%
$$

**Textual logic:** The remaining 8% is bran + germ, which is a useful by-product (sold separately for animal feed, bran oil extraction, or fiber supplements). This is called the **"zero-waste milling"** principle.

#### Step 4 — Calculate Atta:Suji Ratio

Given that atta is 60% of the secondary stream:

$$
M_{\text{atta}} = 0.60 \times 2400 = 1440 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
M_{\text{suji}} = 2400 - 1440 = 960 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Atta : Suji ratio} = 1440 : 960 = 1.5 : 1
$$

**Textual logic:** The mill produces 1.5 tonnes of atta for every 1 tonne of suji. This ratio helps the miller decide market positioning and pricing strategy.

#### Step 5 — Final Mass Balance Summary Table

| Stream | Mass (kg) | % of Input | Commercial Value (₹/kg) | Revenue (₹) |
|--------|-----------|------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| Maida | 6,800 | 68.0% | 38 | 2,58,400 |
| Atta | 1,440 | 14.4% | 35 | 50,400 |
| Suji | 960 | 9.6% | 45 | 43,200 |
| Bran + Germ | 800 | 8.0% | 18 | 14,400 |
| **Total** | **10,000** | **100%** | — | **₹3,66,400** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Value-Addition Insight:** If the same 10,000 kg of clean wheat were sold unprocessed at ₹30/kg, revenue would be ₹3,00,000. After milling, revenue becomes ₹3,66,400 — a **22.1% value addition**, achieved purely through primary processing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Parboiling Mass Balance & Head Rice Yield Improvement

**Problem Context:**
A rice mill parboils **5,000 kg of paddy** at 14% initial moisture. Soaking raises moisture to **30%**. Steam gelatinization adds no net mass. Drying reduces moisture to **13%** final. Calculate: (a) Mass after soaking, (b) Mass after drying, (c) Moisture removed in drying (kg).

#### Step 1 — Mass After Soaking

Soaking is an **additive mass transfer** (water absorption). The dry matter (DM) remains constant:

$$
\text{DM} = M_i \times \left(1 - \dfrac{MC_i}{100}\right) = 5000 \times (1 - 0.14) = 4300 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
M_{\text{soaked}} = \dfrac{\text{DM}}{1 - MC_{\text{soaked}}/100} = \dfrac{4300}{1 - 0.30}
$$

$$
M_{\text{soaked}} = \dfrac{4300}{0.70} = 6142.86 \text{ kg}
$$

**Textual logic:** The paddy absorbed 1142.86 kg of water, swelling by 22.86% in mass.

#### Step 2 — Mass After Drying

During drying, dry matter stays at 4300 kg, but moisture is removed to reach 13% wet basis:

$$
M_{\text{dried}} = \dfrac{\text{DM}}{1 - MC_{\text{final}}/100} = \dfrac{4300}{1 - 0.13}
$$

$$
M_{\text{dried}} = \dfrac{4300}{0.87} = 4942.53 \text{ kg}
$$

**Textual logic:** The dried parboiled paddy weighs 4942.53 kg — very close to the original 5000 kg (1.15% loss is due to husk separation during soaking/handling).

#### Step 3 — Moisture Removed During Drying (Stepwise)

$$
\text{Water}_{\text{removed}} = M_{\text{soaked}} - M_{\text{dried}} = 6142.86 - 4942.53
$$

$$
\text{Water}_{\text{removed}} = 1200.33 \text{ kg}
$$

**Textual logic:** The dryer must evaporate 1200.33 kg of water. The energy requirement can be calculated as:

$$
Q = M_{\text{water}} \times L_v \approx 1200 \times 2260 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.71 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ}
$$

where $L_v$ is the **latent heat of vaporization of water (~2260 kJ/kg at 100 °C)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Puffing Expansion Ratio of Murmura

**Problem Context:**
Raw rice (puffed precursor) has bulk density 0.85 g/cm³. After puffing, bulk density becomes 0.10 g/cm³. Calculate the volumetric expansion ratio.

#### Step 1 — Define Expansion Ratio

$$
ER_{\text{puff}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{raw}}}{\rho_{\text{puffed}}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
ER_{\text{puff}} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.10} = 8.5
$$

**Textual logic:** A single gram of raw rice becomes 8.5 cm³ after puffing. This is a **8.5× volumetric expansion** — a hallmark of well-puffed murmura. Industry standard: **8–12×**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define the term "value addition" in the context of cereal processing. Give two examples each for rice and wheat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Value addition refers to the process of converting raw cereal grains into processed products with enhanced economic value, nutritional quality, shelf life, convenience, and consumer appeal through primary, secondary, and tertiary processing operations.
>
> **Rice examples (1 mark):** (i) Paddy → Parboiled rice (Sella) — nutrient retention; (ii) Paddy → Puffed rice (Murmura) — convenience snack.
>
> **Wheat examples (1 mark):** (i) Wheat grain → Semolina (Suji) — improved culinary utility; (ii) Maida → Biscuits — ready-to-eat convenience.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between parboiled rice and raw (white) rice in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Parboiled Rice | Raw White Rice |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Processing | Soaked, steamed, dried before milling | Soaked and milled directly |
| Nutrient retention | High (B-vitamins migrate to endosperm) | Low (B-vitamins lost in bran) |
| Head Rice Yield | 65–70% (harder kernel) | 55–60% (brittle) |
| Cooking quality | Firmer, less sticky | Softer, more sticky |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B module-level question offers an internal choice. Both options below carry 14 marks, split into 7 + 7.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Treatment**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **manufacturing process of parboiled rice**, with a flow diagram and mass balance. Explain the physico-chemical changes during parboiling. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **production of puffed rice (murmura)**. Describe the principle of puffing, the role of moisture and temperature, and the expansion ratio concept. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Parboiled Rice (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (0.5 mark):**
Parboiling is a hydrothermal treatment in which paddy is soaked, steamed, and dried before milling to gelatinize the starch inside the endosperm and migrate nutrients from the bran to the inner kernel.

**Step 2 — Process Steps (3.5 marks, 0.7 each step):**
1. **Cleaning and Grading:** Paddy is cleaned using air-screen cleaners, magnetic separators, and de-stoners.
2. **Soaking:** Paddy is soaked in water at **50–70 °C for 3–4 hours** until moisture reaches **28–32%**.
3. **Steaming:** Soaked paddy is exposed to **saturated steam at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 15–20 minutes**. This gelatinizes the starch and seals the nutrients inside.
4. **Drying:** The steamed paddy is dried in a **mechanical dryer (LSD / fluidized bed)** to **13–14% moisture** for safe storage and milling.
5. **Milling:** Dried parboiled paddy is dehusked and polished using rubber roll sheller and cone polisher.

**Step 3 — Physico-Chemical Changes (2 marks):**
- **Starch gelatinization:** Crystalline starch converts to amorphous form above 68 °C.
- **Nutrient diffusion:** ~80% of water-soluble B-vitamins and minerals migrate inward.
- **Kernel hardening:** Starch retrogradation makes the kernel **2–3× harder** than raw rice.
- **Pellicle formation:** Bran layer fuses with endosperm, reducing breakage.

**Step 4 — Mass Balance (1 mark):**
For 1000 kg paddy input: clean paddy 990 kg → soaked 1410 kg → cooked 1410 kg → dried 870 kg → parboiled rice 600 kg + husk 200 kg + bran 50 kg + broken 20 kg.

---

**Part (b) — Puffed Rice / Murmura (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Puffed rice is a secondary processed product obtained by rapid heating of pre-conditioned rice (or paddy) at 230–250 °C, causing the internal moisture to vaporize explosively, expanding the kernel to 8–12 times its original volume.

**Step 2 — Raw Material (1 mark):**
Paddy is pre-conditioned to **12–14% moisture** by soaking (1–2 hr) and partial drying. The moisture content is the **critical control parameter** for puffing.

**Step 3 — Process (3 marks):**
1. **Sand/Salt Roasting:** Pre-heated sand or salt is heated to **230–250 °C** in a cast-iron pan.
2. **Puffing Step:** Pre-conditioned paddy (50–100 g batch) is added; stirring is done for **15–20 seconds**.
3. **Popping Action:** Kernel moisture rapidly vaporizes → internal pressure **3–5 atm** → cell wall ruptures → starch granules expand → porous puffed structure forms.
4. **Sieving & Cooling:** Puffed rice is separated from hot sand using a vibrating sieve and cooled.

**Step 4 — Expansion Ratio (2 marks):**
$$
ER_{\text{puff}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{raw}}}{\rho_{\text{puffed}}} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.10} = 8.5
$$
Higher ER indicates better puffing quality. **Factors affecting ER:** moisture content, roasting temperature, time, starch amylose content, and pre-conditioning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Describe the **roller milling process of wheat** with a flow diagram. Explain the production of **maida, suji, and atta** along with their extraction rates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **principle of extrusion cooking** in cereal value addition. Discuss the hardware components of a single-screw extruder and the formation of third-generation snacks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Wheat Milling (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-Milling Preparation (1 mark):**
Cleaned wheat is conditioned (tempered) to **15.5% moisture** by adding water and resting for **18–24 hours** to toughen the bran and soften the endosperm.

**Step 2 — Break System (2 marks):**
Wheat passes through **3–5 break rolls** with corrugated surfaces rotating at differential speeds (2.5:1). Each break roll progressively opens the kernel and scrapes out endosperm chunks. After each break, a **plansifter (sieve stack)** separates:
- **Coarse stock** → next break
- **Semolina/middlings** → reduction rolls
- **Bran** → final bran stream

**Step 3 — Reduction System (2 marks):**
The middlings (semolina) pass through **5–7 reduction rolls with smooth surfaces**, which gradually reduce particle size to flour. **Purifiers** use air aspiration to separate pure endosperm from bran-contaminated particles.

**Step 4 — Products and Extraction Rates (2 marks):**

| Product | Extraction Rate | Particle Size | Application |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Maida (refined flour) | 65–70% | 80–120 µm | Biscuits, bread, cakes |
| Suji (semolina) | 8–10% | 250–500 µm | Halwa, upma, pasta |
| Atta (whole wheat flour) | 15–20% | 150–250 µm | Chapati, roti |
| Bran + Germ | 7–8% | Variable | Animal feed, oil |

**Total edible flour yield: 88–92% of clean wheat.**

---

**Part (b) — Extrusion Cooking (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Principle (1.5 marks):**
Extrusion cooking is a **High Temperature Short Time (HTST)** continuous process in which moistened, expanded starchy and/or proteinaceous food material is forced through a die under high pressure (30–200 atm), temperature (120–200 °C), and shear, causing starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and sudden expansion upon die exit.

**Step 2 — Single-Screw Extruder Components (2.5 marks):**
1. **Feeder:** Metered introduction of preconditioned feed (12–18% moisture).
2. **Barrel:** Outer cylindrical housing with heating/cooling jackets.
3. **Screw:** Rotating helical screw with **compression ratio 2:1 to 3:1**.
4. **Steam Injection Points:** For moisture and temperature control.
5. **Die Plate:** Shapes the product (curls, puffs, third-generation shells).
6. **Cutter Knife:** Rotating blades that cut the extrudate into desired length.
7. **Conditioner:** Pre-conditioning chamber that hydrates and pre-heats feed.

**Step 3 — Process Zones (1.5 marks):**
- **Feed Zone:** Conveying and initial mixing (40–60 °C).
- **Compression Zone:** Material compressed, temperature rises (80–120 °C).
- **Metering/Heating Zone:** High shear and pressure build-up (120–180 °C, 30–100 atm).
- **Die Zone:** Sudden pressure drop → flash evaporation → porous structure.

**Step 4 — Third-Generation Snacks (1.5 marks):**
Third-generation snacks (3G) are produced by re-extruding **pelletized (half-product)** raw materials at low moisture (10–12%) and high temperature (150–170 °C). They exhibit:
- **Low density (0.1–0.4 g/cm³)**
- **Highly porous, crispy texture**
- **High expansion (ER 5–10×)**
- **Hydrolyzed starch and dextrin formation** → improved digestibility and water solubility.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Missing flow diagram** in a "process description" question costs **2 marks minimum** under KTU valuation. Always draw a labeled block diagram.
> 2. **Confusing moisture basis:** KTU expects answers in **wet basis (wb)** by default. Converting between wb and db without stating the basis loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting extraction rates** in wheat milling — students often give only the products, not the **percentage split**, which is the examiner's main check.
> 4. **Oversimplifying parboiling:** Mention **all three** stages (soak, steam, dry) with temperature and time — partial answers score 3/7 maximum.
> 5. **Mixing paddy-to-rice and rice-to-products:** Paddy → Rice is **primary processing**; Rice → Poha/Murmura is **secondary processing**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are merged.
> 6. **Skipping the "why" of puffing:** Just stating "heat the rice" is incomplete. Explain the **moisture-pressure-explosion physics** to score full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 1 — Cereals Value Addition: Last-Minute Recap**

- ✅ **Cereal anatomy = Husk + Bran + Endosperm + Germ** — every processing step modifies one or more of these layers.
- ✅ **Primary processing = Cleaning + Conditioning + Dehusking + Milling** — the "raw → edible grain" transformation.
- ✅ **Secondary processing = Flour/Flake/Puff/Oil** — the "grain → intermediate product" transformation.
- ✅ **Tertiary processing = Bakery/Pasta/RTE/Mixes** — the "intermediate → consumer product" transformation.
- ✅ **Parboiling improves HRY from 55% to 65–70%** — this is a KTU-favorite numerical example.
- ✅ **Wheat extraction hierarchy: Bran (7%) < Maida (68%) < Suji (10%) < Atta (15%)** — remember the 7-68-10-15 sequence.
- ✅ **Puffing ER = 8–12×** for murmura; uses moisture + heat + sudden pressure release.
- ✅ **Rice bran oil** — extracted with **n-hexane (BP 68.7 °C)**, yield **18–22%**, rich in **γ-oryzanol + tocotrienols**.
- ✅ **Gluten = Gliadin + Glutenin** — only wheat has functional gluten; rice, maize, and millets are gluten-free.
- ✅ **Gelatinization temperature**: Rice **68–78 °C**, Wheat **58–65 °C**, Maize **62–72 °C**.
- ✅ **Extrusion principle: HTST (High Temperature Short Time)** — combines cooking, forming, and puffing in one operation.
- ✅ **Value-addition rule of thumb:** Each processing step adds **1.5×–3× value**; specialty/export products reach **10×–25×**.
- ✅ **Atta vs Maida:** Atta is **95–98% extraction** (whole grain); Maida is **65–70% extraction** (endosperm only).
- ✅ **FSSAI standards** to remember: Maida ash content **≤ 0.7%**, Moisture **≤ 14%**, Acid-insoluble ash **≤ 0.05%**.
- ✅ **"Zero-waste milling"** is achievable — bran (feed/oil), germ (oil/vitamin E), broken rice (flour/snacks) all become revenue streams.
- ✅ **Head Rice Yield (HRY) formula** = (whole kernel mass / paddy input mass) × 100 — frequently asked in numerical problems.
- ✅ **WAI/WSI** are extrusion quality parameters — high WAI = good water uptake; high WSI = starch degradation (controlled dextrinization).
- ✅ **Gluten yield of 25–35%** in hard wheat → suitable for bread; **<25%** → suitable for biscuits and cakes.
- ✅ **Rice flakes (Poha)** process = Soak (2 hr) → Roast (1 min) → Flatten using roller → Dry. The poha yields ~70% of paddy mass.
- ✅ **Third-generation (3G) snacks** = re-extruded pellets, ultra-low density (0.1–0.4 g/cm³), the "king of snack engineering."
- ✅ **Always mention flow diagrams** in Part B process questions — 2 marks saved on every KTU answer script.

# 🍿 MODULE 3: SNACK FOODS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAGT862 – Development of Processed Food Products**, **Snack Foods** are defined as:

> **Snack Foods:** A category of convenience food products formulated to be consumed between main meals, characterized by specific textural attributes (crispness, crunchiness), controlled moisture content (**< 4% for fried/extruded snacks** and **6–10% for baked snacks**), extended shelf life, and ready-to-eat (RTE) convenience. They are produced through controlled unit operations such as **extrusion cooking, deep-fat frying, baking, popping, pelletization, or flaking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, Unit 3):**
> The 2024 scheme specifically targets the following sub-topics for ESE (End Semester Examination):
> 1. Classification of snack foods
> 2. Extruded snacks — raw materials, process, twin-screw vs single-screw
> 3. Pellet snacks (3D snacks) — formulation and processing
> 4. Popcorn and puffed snacks
> 5. Coated and flavored snacks
> 6. Quality parameters, packaging, and shelf-life

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your regular home-cooked meal — it requires preparation time, utensils, and a table setting. Now think of the small packets of food you can tear open, eat immediately with your fingers, and feel instant satisfaction. That is the world of snack foods.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a snack food factory like a controlled kitchen at industrial scale. The **dough** is your raw material (much like chapati dough), but instead of rolling and roasting on a tawa, it is forced under high pressure and temperature through a die — like squeezing toothpaste — and the sudden pressure drop causes the snack to puff up (like a balloon expanding when you release the nozzle). The water inside instantly flashes to steam, creating the airy, crunchy texture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Process Flow (Mental Model):**
> Raw Grains/Cereals → Milling → Mixing (with water, salt, flavor) → Cooking (heat + pressure) → Shaping/Expanding → Drying/Frying → Seasoning → Packaging

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Snack Food Science

Any snack food product is engineered by controlling three fundamental pillars:

1. **Pillar 1 — Material (Raw Material Composition):** Starch, protein, fiber, and moisture content of the base material.
2. **Pillar 2 — Process (Unit Operation):** Type of cooking (extrusion, frying, baking, popping).
3. **Pillar 3 — Texture & Sensory (Quality Attribute):** Crispness, expansion ratio, bulk density, color, and flavor.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Reference with COs (Course Outcomes):**
> - **CO3:** "Apply process technology for development of various snack food products" — *This note directly addresses this CO.*
> - **CO4:** "Evaluate quality and shelf-life of processed products" — *Quality parameters and packaging are covered.*

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Moisture Content (Extruded)** | **< 4%** | Microbial safety threshold |
| **Moisture Content (Baked)** | **6–10%** | Acceptable crispness |
| **Water Activity ($a_w$)** | **< 0.6** | Shelf stability |
| **Frying Temperature** | **150–190 °C** | Standard industrial fryer |
| **Extrusion Temperature** | **120–200 °C** | Cooking zone range |
| **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | **3–15** | Measure of puffing |
| **Bulk Density** | **0.1–0.5 g/cm³** | Light, airy snack |
| **Screw Speed (Extruder)** | **200–500 rpm** | Twin-screw range |
| **Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$)** | **~ 50–80 °C** for starches | Texture transition |
| **Residence Time in Extruder** | **10–60 seconds** | Short-time cooking |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Snack Foods (Syllabus Mandate)

Snack foods are broadly classified into **six industrial categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Focus:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the following six classes. Memorize the example of each:

1. **Extruded Snacks** — *Example: Cheese balls, Kurkure, puffs (direct-expanded)*
2. **Pellet Snacks (3D Snacks)** — *Example: 3D pellet-based rings, stars, balls (half-products)*
3. **Popped / Puffed Snacks** — *Example: Popcorn, puffed rice (murmura), corn pops*
4. **Coated & Fried Snacks** — *Example: Potato chips, banana chips, namkeen*
5. **Baked Snacks** — *Example: Baked crackers, cookies, khakhra*
6. **Flaked & Toasted Snacks** — *Example: Corn flakes, chivda, baked sev*

A schematic tree is provided in **Section 4**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Sub-Topic Breakdown

### **A. EXTRUDED SNACKS — The Heart of Modern Snack Industry**

#### A.1 Definition
Extrusion cooking is a **High Temperature Short Time (HTST)** process in which moistened, starchy, and proteinaceous food material is forced through a die under controlled conditions of **temperature (120–200 °C)**, **pressure (20–150 bar)**, and **shear**, resulting in a plasticized melt that expands upon exiting the die.

#### A.2 The Four Operational Zones Inside an Extruder

The extruder barrel is conceptually divided into **four sequential zones**:

| Zone | Function | Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Feed Zone** | Receives raw material via hopper | Low temp, conveying |
| **2. Mixing / Kneading Zone** | Hydrates and mixes ingredients | 60–90 °C |
| **3. Cooking Zone** | Starch gelatinization, protein denaturation | 100–150 °C |
| **4. Die / Expansion Zone** | Final shaping and puffing | 150–200 °C, high pressure |

#### A.3 Single-Screw vs Twin-Screw Extruder (Critical for KTU)

| Feature | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conveying Mechanism** | Drag flow (passive) | Positive displacement (active) |
| **Mixing Efficiency** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Residence Time Distribution** | Wide | Narrow & uniform |
| **Heat Transfer** | Limited | High (self-wiping) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High |
| **Best For** | Simple direct expansion | Co-extrusion, complex formulations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Expectation:** Students must be able to **compare and contrast** single vs twin-screw extruders — a guaranteed 7-mark question topic.

#### A.4 Physico-Chemical Changes During Extrusion
- **Starch Gelatinization:** Orderly starch granules absorb water and lose crystallinity.
- **Protein Denaturation:** Unfolding of protein structures.
- **Maillard Reaction:** Between reducing sugars and amino groups (gives golden color).
- **Retrogradation Suppression:** HTST minimizes post-process hardening.

---

### **B. PELLET SNACKS (3D SNACKS) — The Half-Product Innovation**

#### B.1 Concept
A **pellet snack** is a **half-finished product** manufactured at a central plant, then shipped to **satellite plants** where it is fried or expanded by the consumer-distributor. This separation of *cooking* and *expansion* is unique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The 3D pellet (ring/star/ball shape) is dense and hard after extrusion at the factory. At the local fryer outlet, it is dropped in hot oil (180 °C, 8–12 seconds), and the trapped moisture inside instantly vaporizes → the pellet puffs up into a 3D snack.

#### B.2 Formulation Principles
- **High starch content** (corn grits, tapioca, potato) — provides puffability.
- **Low moisture** (10–14%) — enables rapid steam generation on frying.
- **Cellulating agents** — pre-gelatinized starch creates gas cells.
- **Bland base flavor** — allows oil absorption and seasoning coating.

#### B.3 Processing Steps
1. Dry-mix ingredients in a planetary or ribbon blender.
2. Feed into low-shear extruder (cooler than direct expansion: 70–100 °C).
3. Extrude through shaped die (ring, star, ball).
4. Dry pellets to **8–12% moisture**.
5. Pack in moisture-barrier pouches.
6. At retail/fryer: **fry at 175–185 °C for 8–15 s** → expansion ratio 6–12×.

---

### **C. POPPED / PUFFED SNACKS**

#### C.1 Popcorn Mechanism
Each popcorn kernel has a **pericarp** (hard outer shell) and a starchy endosperm containing 13–14% moisture. Upon heating:
- Internal water turns to steam (pressure builds to **~ 135 psi** inside kernel).
- The pericarp ruptures.
- Starch granules expand rapidly → fluffy popped corn.

**Optimal popping temperature:** **180–200 °C**.
**Popping yield:** **~ 98–99%** (industrial standard).
**Expansion ratio:** **30–40×** kernel volume.

#### C.2 Puffed Rice (Murmura)
Produced by **hot sand / hot air puffing (gun puffing)** — pre-conditioned rice grains (12–14% moisture) are rapidly heated → internal steam expansion → puffing.

---

### **D. COATED & FRIED SNACKS**

#### D.1 Deep-Fat Frying Physics
Frying is simultaneous heat and mass transfer. The governing rate equation is:

$$ J_w = \frac{k_m \cdot (a_w^{\text{surface}} - a_w^{\text{core}})}{L} $$

Where:
- $J_w$ = water flux (kg water / m²·s)
- $k_m$ = mass transfer coefficient
- $a_w^{\text{surface}}$ = water activity at surface
- $a_w^{\text{core}}$ = water activity at core
- $L$ = characteristic thickness (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does frying work?** Water inside the snack boils at ~ 100 °C, while the oil is at 150–190 °C. The escaping steam creates a **porous internal structure**, while the dehydrated outer surface becomes crisp and golden due to the **Maillard reaction**.

#### D.2 Oil Uptake Mechanism
Oil enters the snack via **two pathways**:
1. **Surface adhesion** (during removal from oil).
2. **Capillary suction** during cooling — as steam escapes and condenses, a pressure differential draws oil into the porous structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Mitigation:** Pre-drying the product, using **hydrocolloid coatings** (methylcellulose, pectin), and using **vacuum frying** significantly reduce oil uptake.

---

### **E. BAKED SNACKS**

#### E.1 Why Bake?
Baking offers a **healthier alternative** to frying (lower fat content: 4–10% vs 20–35%). However, baked snacks have a **denser texture** and require precise humidity control.

#### E.2 Parameters
- **Oven temperature:** 160–220 °C
- **Baking time:** 5–15 minutes
- **Final moisture:** 6–10%
- **Dough moisture (pre-bake):** 28–35%

## 2.3 The "WHY–HOW" Framework (Engineering Logic)

**Q: Why is twin-screw extrusion preferred for complex snack formulations?**
**A (HOW):** Two intermeshing screws provide *self-wiping* action → continuous renewal of barrel surface → uniform heat transfer.
**A (WHY):** Starch-based mixtures with high fat/protein require homogeneous cooking. Single-screw cannot achieve this without long residence time, causing burnt patches.

**Q: Why do pellets need pre-conditioning?**
**A (HOW):** Pellets are manufactured with low moisture (10–14%) and shipped dense. The fryer outlet conditions the pellet by immersion in hot oil.
**A (WHY):** This **decouples the two HTST steps** (cooking vs expansion) — reduces distribution cost (lighter than expanded snacks) and allows **fresh frying at point-of-sale**.

**Q: Why is oil uptake high in fried snacks?**
**A (HOW):** As the snack cools, internal steam condenses, creating partial vacuum. Capillary action draws oil into the porous matrix.
**A (WHY):** Two industrial countermeasures:
1. **Pre-dry** the product surface.
2. **Apply hydrocolloid edible coating** — forms a moisture/oil barrier film.

## 2.5 Glass Transition Theory — The Texture Determinant

The crispness of a snack is governed by whether the product temperature is **above or below** its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**. A snack is **crisp** when its moisture and temperature place it in the **glassy state** (below $T_g$).

$$ T_g \approx T_g^{\text{dry}} - k \cdot W $$

Where:
- $T_g^{\text{dry}}$ = glass transition temp of dry material (e.g., ~ 220 °C for starch)
- $k$ = plasticizing constant (~ 10 °C per % moisture)
- $W$ = moisture content (% w/w)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** A snack stored in humid conditions absorbs moisture → $T_g$ drops → at room temperature, the snack may transition from glassy to rubbery state → **loss of crispness** (the "soggy chip" problem).
